scenario: Being invited to a churrasco and asking what to bring

transcript.text: Vou fazer um churrasco no sábado. Você vem?
Vou sim! O que eu levo?
Eu levo a carne. Cada um leva o seu, então pode levar bebida.
Posso levar cerveja e um refrigerante também.
Perfeito. Chega lá umas duas da tarde.
Combinado. Até sábado!

translation.text: I'm having a barbecue on Saturday. Are you coming?
Yes, I am! What should I bring?
I've got the meat covered. Everyone brings their own, so you could bring drinks.
I can bring beer and a soda too.
Perfect. Show up around two in the afternoon.
Deal. See you Saturday!

grammarNotes: "Cada um leva o seu" is the standard way to signal the unwritten rule of Brazilian barbecues: the host usually covers the meat, guests cover their own drinks.

scenario: Ordering meat doneness at the grill

transcript.text: Qual é o seu ponto da carne?
Pra mim, mal passado, por favor.
E pro seu amigo?
Ele gosta bem passado.
Tá bom, já corto um pedaço ao ponto pra vocês provarem também.
Beleza, obrigado!

translation.text: What doneness do you want your meat?
Rare for me, please.
And for your friend?
He likes it well done.
Okay, I'll also cut a medium piece for you two to try.
Cool, thanks!

grammarNotes: At a churrasco the churrasqueiro often slices straight off the skewer table-side, asking each guest's ponto individually rather than cooking everything to one doneness.

scenario: Complimenting the churrasqueiro on the meat

transcript.text: Que delícia essa picanha!
Foi o Marcos que fez, viu?
Mandou bem na carne hoje!
Ele vai adorar ouvir isso.

translation.text: This picanha is so good!
Marcos made it, you know.
He did a great job with the meat today!
He's going to love hearing that.

grammarNotes: Praise usually goes straight to whoever manned the grill, not just "the food" in general — the churrasqueiro's skill is part of the social credit of hosting.

scenario: Asking about a specific cut of meat on the grill

transcript.text: O que é esse corte aí, com aquela capa de gordura?
Isso é maminha. É mais leve que a picanha.
E aquele ali, mais escuro?
Cupim. Ele fica umas quatro horas no fogo antes de ficar macio.
Nossa, vale a pena esperar?
Vale sim, desmancha na boca.

translation.text: What cut is that one, with that fat cap?
That's maminha. It's lighter than picanha.
And that one over there, the darker one?
Cupim. It sits on the fire for about four hours before it gets tender.
Wow, is it worth the wait?
It is, it falls apart in your mouth.

scenario: Offering to help set up and later clean up

transcript.text: Cheguei cedo. Posso ajudar a arrumar a mesa?
Pode sim, os pratos estão na cozinha.
E o carvão, já acendeu o fogo?
Ainda não, vou acender agora.
No final eu ajudo a limpar também, pode deixar.
Você é gente boa, viu?

translation.text: I got here early. Can I help set up the table?
Sure, the plates are in the kitchen.
And the charcoal, have you lit the fire already?
Not yet, I'm about to light it.
At the end I'll help clean up too, don't worry about it.
You're a good one, you know that?

grammarNotes: "Pode deixar" (literally "you can leave it [to me]") is a very common informal way to reassure someone you've got something handled.

scenario: Small talk while waiting for the meat to be ready

transcript.text: Quanto tempo falta pra carne ficar pronta?
Uns vinte minutos, acho. Quer um espetinho de queijo coalho enquanto isso?
Quero sim, tô com fome já.
Passa o molho de pimenta pra mim, por favor.
Toma. Esse churrasco tá ótimo, viu?
Tá mesmo. E ainda nem chegou a fraldinha.

translation.text: How much longer until the meat's ready?
About twenty minutes, I think. Want a grilled cheese skewer in the meantime?
Yes please, I'm already hungry.
Pass me the hot sauce, please.
Here. This barbecue is great, you know?
It really is. And the fraldinha hasn't even come out yet.
